answers.q: (1) Exact end of the JobType union and SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES array — where to append the three new types
answers.answer:
File: /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/src/lib/unified-job-queue.ts

There are TWO places that must be kept in sync — the `JobType` union (lines 25-41) AND the `SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES` runtime array (lines 43-60). If you only add to the union, the job will type-check but claim_jobs / getNextPendingJob / resetStaleJobs will silently ignore it (they filter `.in('type', SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES)`), so the job will sit pending forever.

A) JobType union — last member is on line 41, closing at end of line 41:
```
25	export type JobType =
26	  | 'send_sms'
...
40	  | 'refresh_receipt_duplicate_candidates'
41	  | 'reconcile_receipt_invoice_payments'
```
Append the three new `| 'your_type'` lines AFTER line 41 (before the `SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES` const on line 43). Each is `  | 'new_type'`.

B) SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES array — last element is line 59, array closes on line 60:
```
43	const SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES: JobType[] = [
44	  'send_sms',
...
58	  'refresh_receipt_duplicate_candidates',
59	  'reconcile_receipt_invoice_payments',
60	]
```
Insert the three new `'new_type',` entries after line 59, before the closing `]` on line 60. Note line 59 already has a trailing comma, so just add three more comma-terminated string lines.

Optional: `JOB_TIMEOUTS_MS` (lines 74-79) is a `Partial<Record<JobType, number>>` — only list a type here if you want a custom/disabled timeout (`0` means no timeout). Types not listed use `DEFAULT_JOB_TIMEOUT_MS` (120000ms, line 65-67). You do NOT need to add entries here unless your handler runs longer than 120s.

answers.q: (2) executeJob switch: 2-3 existing case blocks in full, showing handler naming/calling convention and the result shape
answers.answer:
File: /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/src/lib/unified-job-queue.ts. The switch is `private async executeJob(type: JobType, payload: JobPayload): Promise<any>` starting line 985. Handlers are NOT methods — each case dynamically `import()`s the real handler (comment line 986: "Import handlers dynamically to avoid circular dependencies") and returns its result. The returned value becomes the job row's `result` column (persisted on completion, line 836). Return any JSON-serialisable object; there is no required result shape. `payload` is the enqueued payload plus an injected `__job_id` string (added at line 821: `{ ...job.payload, __job_id: job.id }`).

Example A — simplest, no-arg service delegation (lines 1004-1012):
```
1004	      case 'detect_receipt_rule_conflicts': {
1005	        const { performDetectReceiptRuleConflicts } = await import('@/services/receipts')
1006	        return performDetectReceiptRuleConflicts()
1007	      }
1008
1009	      case 'suggest_receipt_rules': {
1010	        const { performSuggestReceiptRules } = await import('@/services/receipts')
1011	        return performSuggestReceiptRules()
1012	      }
```

Example B — validates payload, calls admin client, returns a count object (lines 988-1002):
```
988	      case 'classify_receipt_transactions': {
989	        const transactionIds = Array.isArray(payload.transactionIds)
990	          ? payload.transactionIds.filter((id) => typeof id === 'string' && id.length > 0)
991	          : []
992
993	        if (!transactionIds.length) {
994	          return { skipped: true }
995	        }
996
997	        const { classifyReceiptTransactionsWithAI } = await import('@/lib/receipts/ai-classification')
998	        const supabase = createAdminClient()
999
1000	        await classifyReceiptTransactionsWithAI(supabase, transactionIds)
1001	        return { processed: transactionIds.length }
1002	      }
```

Example C — a handler that returns an envelope and the case converts an error field into a throw (lines 1014-1021):
```
1014	      case 'refresh_receipt_duplicate_candidates': {
1015	        const { performRefreshReceiptDuplicateCandidates } = await import('@/services/receipts')
1016	        const result = await performRefreshReceiptDuplicateCandidates()
1017	        if (result.error) {
1018	          throw new Error(result.error)
1019	        }
1020	        return result
1021	      }
```

Example D — payload-typed delegation extracting fields then calling a lib dispatcher (lines 1142-1165):
```
1142	      case 'send_event_reschedule_notifications': {
1143	        const eventId = typeof payload.eventId === 'string' ? payload.eventId : ''
...
1151	        if (!eventId || !newDate || !userId) {
1152	          throw new Error('send_event_reschedule_notifications job blocked: missing required payload')
1153	        }
1154
1155	        const { dispatchEventRescheduleNotifications } = await import('@/lib/events/reschedule-notifications')
1156	        return dispatchEventRescheduleNotifications({ eventId, eventName, oldDate, oldTime, newDate, newTime, userId })
1157	      }
```

WHERE TO APPEND new cases: add them inside the switch before the `default` clause. The `default` is at lines 1225-1226:
```
1225	      default:
1226	        throw new Error(`Unknown job type: ${type}`)
```
The last real case before default is `export_employees` (lines 1221-1223), which is a non-braced case — note it uses NO block braces and would leak `const` into following cases if another case followed without braces, so ALWAYS wrap new cases in `{ ... }` blocks like examples A-D:
```
1221	      case 'export_employees':
1222	        const { exportEmployees } = await import('@/app/actions/employeeExport')
1223	        return await exportEmployees(payload.filters || {})
```
Insert new `case 'new_type': { ... }` blocks between line 1223 and the `default:` on line 1225.

answers.q: (3) enqueue() method signature, JobOptions (priority, maxAttempts, delay, unique), and real example calls
answers.answer:
Signature (lines 212-216):
```
212	  async enqueue(
213	    type: JobType,
214	    payload: JobPayload,
215	    options: JobOptions = {}
216	  ): Promise<{ success: boolean; jobId?: string; error?: string }> {
```

JobOptions interface (lines 190-195):
```
190	export interface JobOptions {
191	  priority?: number
192	  maxAttempts?: number
193	  delay?: number // milliseconds
194	  unique?: string // unique key to prevent duplicates
195	}
```

How options map to the inserted row (lines 344-354):
- `priority`: defaults to 0 (`options.priority || 0`). Higher = processed first (claim/order is `priority DESC, created_at ASC`). Observed values in real code: 30 (cancel), 20 (reschedule), -10 (receipt system jobs).
- `maxAttempts`: defaults to 3 (`options.maxAttempts || 3`). Controls retry count with exponential backoff (`2^attempt * 60000ms`).
- `delay`: milliseconds; sets `scheduled_for = new Date(Date.now() + options.delay)`; omitted = now.
- `unique`: dedupe key. When set, it is stored INTO the payload as `unique_key` (line 346: `{ ...payload, unique_key: options.unique }`) and drives an idempotency lock plus a pending/processing existence check; a duplicate returns `{ success: true, jobId: <existing> }` without inserting.

Real example calls (import is `import { jobQueue } from '@/lib/unified-job-queue'`):

A) events.ts:474 (priority + maxAttempts + unique):
```
const enqueueResult = await jobQueue.enqueue(
  'send_event_reschedule_notifications',
  { eventId: id, eventName: ..., oldDate: _oldDate, oldTime: _oldTime, newDate: ..., newTime: ..., userId: user.id },
  { priority: 20, maxAttempts: 3, unique: `event_reschedule:${id}:${_oldDate ?? ''}:${_oldTime ?? ''}:${newDate ?? ''}:${newTime ?? ''}` },
)
```

B) events.ts:505 (cancel, priority 30):
```
await jobQueue.enqueue('cancel_event_bookings',
  { eventId: id, eventName: ..., eventDate: ..., eventTime: ..., cancelledBy: user.id },
  { priority: 30, maxAttempts: 3, unique: `cancel_event_bookings:${id}` })
```

C) event-check-in.ts:791 (delay + unique, no priority):
```
await jobQueue.enqueue('send_sms', {
  to: input.phone, message, customerId: input.customerId,
  metadata: { event_id: input.event.id, event_check_in_id: input.checkInId, template_key: 'event_check_in_thank_you' },
}, { delay: buildThankYouDelayMs(input.event), unique: `event_check_in_thank_you:${input.checkInId}` })
```

D) receipts/receiptMutations.ts:77 (negative priority + unique, generic wrapper — also shows how to type the `type` param generically):
```
const result = await jobQueue.enqueue(type, payload, { priority: -10, unique: `receipts:${type}:${uniqueSuffix}` })
```
where `type: Parameters<typeof jobQueue.enqueue>[0]` (receiptMutations.ts:73).

E) receiptMutations.ts:627 (minimal, no options at all):
```
jobQueue.enqueue('classify_receipt_transactions', { transactionIds })
```

answers.q: (4) How a handler reports success/failure — throw vs return {success}?
answers.answer:
Handlers report FAILURE by THROWING; they report SUCCESS by RETURNING (any value). There is no `{ success }` contract at the handler/executeJob layer — the boolean-envelope pattern (`{ success, error }`) is only the enqueue() return type, not the handler return type.

Mechanics in processJob (lines 819-880): the case's returned value is awaited into `result` (line 825) and written to the job row's `result` column on completion (line 836). If the handler (or the dynamically-imported function it calls) throws, the catch block (starts line 881) marks the job `failed` (or re-queues `pending` with exponential backoff if `attempts < max_attempts`, lines 887-905) using `error.message` as `error_message`.

Consequence for the envelope pattern: several handlers call functions that return `{ error?: string }` and must manually convert that into a throw, or the job is wrongly marked completed. See refresh_receipt_duplicate_candidates (lines 1016-1020): `if (result.error) { throw new Error(result.error) }`. Same for send_bulk_sms (lines 1127-1138) and send_sms (lines 1088-1098) which throw on `!result.success`. So: if your new handler delegates to a server-action-style function returning `{ success?, error? }`, you MUST inspect it and `throw new Error(result.error)` on failure, then `return result` on success.

Special case (SMS only, not needed for generic jobs): throwing a `FatalSmsSafetyError` (class at lines 146-156, codes 'logging_failed' | 'safety_unavailable' | 'idempotency_conflict') signals a fatal safety failure that aborts the remaining serial SMS batch. This machinery is SMS-specific (guarded by `job.type === 'send_sms' || 'send_bulk_sms'` throughout processJob) — for ordinary new job types you just throw a plain `new Error(...)`.

Validation/guard convention inside cases: throw a descriptive Error on bad payload, e.g. line 1152 `throw new Error('send_event_reschedule_notifications job blocked: missing required payload')`. Or `return { skipped: true }` (line 994) if a no-op should count as success rather than failure.

answers.q: (5) Singleton export (jobQueue) or class? The import other files use
answers.answer:
BOTH exist. The class is exported (`export class UnifiedJobQueue` line 197) with a private constructor (line 200) and a `static getInstance()` (lines 202-207), but it is a singleton — the module exports a ready-made instance at the very bottom:
```
1315	// Export singleton instance
1316	export const jobQueue = UnifiedJobQueue.getInstance()
```

Every caller imports the singleton, NOT the class. Canonical import (used by sms-bulk-direct.ts:7, events.ts:64, job-queue.ts:4, event-check-in.ts:13, api/jobs/process/route.ts:2, api/jobs/process-now/route.ts:2, services/receipts/receiptMutations.ts:17):
```
import { jobQueue } from '@/lib/unified-job-queue'
```
One dynamic-import site (twilio.ts:465) does: `const { jobQueue } = await import('@/lib/unified-job-queue')`.

Type note: `JobType` and `JobOptions` are also exported from this module if you need to reference them. Tests mock the module like `vi.mock('@/lib/unified-job-queue', () => ({ ... }))` (refundEventBookingManual.test.ts:66). For the three new job types you do NOT touch this export — you add types + a case, and callers keep using the same `jobQueue` singleton.

answers.q: (6) claim_jobs / SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES filtering around line 642
answers.answer:
processJobs (lines 636-643) calls the private claimJobs helper, passing the whole SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES array as the `types` filter:
```
636	  async processJobs(limit = 10): Promise<void> {
637	    const supabase = await createAdminClient()
638	    await this.resetStaleJobs(supabase)
639
640	    const jobs = await this.claimJobs(supabase, {
641	      limit,
642	      types: SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES,
643	    })
```

claimJobs (lines 445-473) invokes the Postgres RPC `claim_jobs` with `batch_size`, `job_types` (the SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES array), and `lease_seconds`:
```
445	  private async claimJobs(
446	    supabase: ReturnType<typeof createAdminClient>,
447	    options: { limit: number; types: JobType[] }
448	  ): Promise<Job[]> {
449	    if (options.limit <= 0) { return [] }
453	    const { data, error } = await supabase.rpc('claim_jobs', {
454	      batch_size: options.limit,
455	      job_types: options.types,
456	      lease_seconds: DEFAULT_LEASE_SECONDS,
457	    })
```
If the RPC is missing (error.code PGRST202 or message includes 'claim_jobs', lines 462-468) it falls back to claimJobsFallback (lines 475-540) which does a direct `.from('jobs').select().eq('status','pending').in('type', options.types)` update loop.

The SAME SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES filter is also applied in getNextPendingJob (line 430: `.in('type', SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES)`) and resetStaleJobs (line 551: `.in('type', SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES)`). 

KEY IMPLICATION for adding job types: because claim/next/stale-reset all filter `.in('type', SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES)` (both the RPC path via `job_types` and the fallback), a new job type that is added to the JobType union but NOT to the SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES array (lines 43-60) will be enqueued fine but NEVER claimed or processed — it sits pending indefinitely. There is also a DB-side function `claim_jobs` (in supabase/migrations) that receives `job_types`; since the caller passes the array explicitly there is no hardcoded type list to change inside the RPC, but confirm the `jobs.type` column/enum (if it is a CHECK/enum constraint rather than free text) accepts the new string values — grep supabase/migrations for the jobs table definition before enqueuing a brand-new type in production.

answers.q: (1) A COMPLETE simple cron route file end to end, showing imports, authorizeCronRequest usage, the London-time gate, and how it does work + the response.
(2) The exact vercel.json cron entry format and where crons are listed.

(1) Quote src/lib/foh/user-mode.ts in FULL
(2) Exact FOH_MODULES change needed + the two consuming gates in AuthenticatedLayout.tsx (isFohPath :35, redirect effect :92-100, render gate :130)
(3) Quote src/lib/foh/api-auth.ts:15-30 (the 403 gate)
(4) FohHeader action-row buttons (Walk-in / Add booking) verbatim + insertion point + props/state in scope
answers.answer:
File: /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/components/FohHeader.tsx ('use client', line 1). The Walk-in / Add booking pair lives inside a `canEdit &&` fragment at lines 269-296, in the right-hand action row (`<div className="flex flex-wrap items-center gap-2">` opened at line 225). VERBATIM (lines 269-296):

```tsx
          {canEdit && (
            <>
              <button
                type="button"
                onClick={() => openNowCreateModal('walk_in')}
                className={cn(
                  'rounded-md px-4 py-2 text-sm text-white',
                  isManagerKioskStyle
                    ? 'bg-sidebar px-2.5 py-1 text-[11px] font-semibold hover:bg-green-700'
                    : 'bg-sidebar font-medium hover:bg-sidebar/90'
                )}
              >
                Walk-in
              </button>
              <button
                type="button"
                onClick={() => openNowCreateModal('booking')}
                className={cn(
                  'rounded-md border border-gray-300 bg-white px-4 py-2 text-sm font-medium text-gray-700 hover:bg-gray-50',
                  isManagerKioskStyle
                    ? 'px-2.5 py-1 text-[11px]'
                    : ''
                )}
              >
                Add booking
              </button>
            </>
          )}
```

These are RAW `<button>` elements (NOT `@/ds` Button) styled directly with Tailwind + `cn(...)`. Match that pattern for a new sibling button. Primary/green style = `'rounded-md px-4 py-2 text-sm text-white'` + `'bg-sidebar font-medium hover:bg-sidebar/90'` (kiosk: `'bg-sidebar px-2.5 py-1 text-[11px] font-semibold hover:bg-green-700'`). Secondary/white style = `'rounded-md border border-gray-300 bg-white px-4 py-2 text-sm font-medium text-gray-700 hover:bg-gray-50'` (kiosk shrink: `'px-2.5 py-1 text-[11px]'`).

INSERTION POINT: add a new `<button>` immediately after the Add booking button (after line 294 `</button>`, before line 295 `</>`) to keep it inside the same `canEdit` gate; or place it after the whole `canEdit && (...)` fragment at line 296 if it should show regardless of edit rights (mirror the view-toggle comment at line 249: "always visible ... not gated by canEdit").

PROPS/STATE IN SCOPE inside the component (destructured from props at lines 36-54, plus locals at 56-63): `canEdit` (boolean, gates the fragment), `styleVariant` and its derived `isManagerKioskStyle = styleVariant === 'manager_kiosk'` (line 56 — use for kiosk-compact classes), `date`, `setDate`, `clockNow`, `londonTodayIso` (line 57), `viewingToday` (line 58), `totals`, `viewMode`, `outsideCount`, `onViewModeChange`, `nextUpcomingEvent`, `upcomingEventsLoaded`, `submittingFoodOrderAlert`, `statusMessage`, `errorMessage`, `lastInteractionAtMsRef` (React.MutableRefObject<number> — set `.current = Date.now()` to reset the 5-min idle timer), `onSendFoodOrderAlert`, `onOpenCreateModal`. Local helper `openNowCreateModal(mode)` (lines 59-63) sets date to today, stamps the idle ref, then calls `onOpenCreateModal({ mode })`. `cn` is imported from `@/lib/utils` (line 5). There is NO local click handler for checklists — you'd add a new prop (e.g. `onOpenChecklist`) to `FohHeaderProps` (lines 9-33) and thread it from FohScheduleClient.

answers.q: (5) How FOH page.tsx passes data to FohHeader; server or client component
(1) Full quote of src/app/(authenticated)/cashing-up/layout.tsx (PageHeader + SectionNav + permission redirect)
(2) A bare server page under cashing-up: how it fetches data via an action/service and renders a client component
(3) Imports/props of PageHeader and SectionNav from @/ds
answers.answer:
IMPORT (both come from the barrel): `import { PageHeader, SectionNav } from '@/ds'` Source files: PageHeader at /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/src/ds/composites/PageHeader.tsx, SectionNav at /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/src/ds/composites/SectionNav.tsx. Both are re-exported from src/ds/composites/index.ts (lines 2-3: `export { PageHeader } from './PageHeader'` / `export { SectionNav } from './SectionNav'`), which the top-level `@/ds` barrel re-exports. --- PageHeader (SERVER component, no 'use client') props --- ```ts interface Breadcrumb { label: string href?: string } interface PageHeaderProps { breadcrumbs?: Breadcrumb[] // optional trail; last item rendered as current (no link) title: string // required subtitle?: string // optional line under the title actions?: React.ReactNode // right-aligned action buttons on the title row className?: string } ``` Renders an `

` with optional subtitle `

`. Wrapper is `

`. Because it is a plain server component you can render it directly in a server layout/page. Usage in cashing-up: ``. --- SectionNav ('use client' component) props --- ```ts interface SectionNavItem { id: string label: string count?: number badge?: string | number href?: string // if set -> renders a next/link (page navigation, the pattern cashing-up uses) icon?: React.ReactNode disabled?: boolean } export interface SectionNavProps { items: SectionNavItem[] activeId: string // required; empty string '' = no active tab onSelect?: (id: string) => void // only used for button/in-page-tab variant (items without href) className?: string } ``` Behaviour: each item with an `href` renders as a `next/link` tab (role="tab", aria-current when active); items without `href` render as `

answers.q: (4) How checkUserPermission is called in a layout/page, and how redirect('/unauthorized') is used
answers.answer:
IMPORTS used in the layout:
```tsx
import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
import { checkUserPermission } from '@/app/actions/rbac'
```

CALL PATTERN (from cashing-up/layout.tsx, an async server component):
```tsx
const canView = await checkUserPermission('cashing_up', 'view')
if (!canView) redirect('/unauthorized')
```

SIGNATURE of checkUserPermission (src/app/actions/rbac.ts, line 64):
```ts
export async function checkUserPermission(
  moduleName: ModuleName,
  action: ActionType,
  userId?: string
): Promise<boolean>
```
- It is a server action ('use server' file). Returns `true`/`false` (never throws for a denied user; returns false). When `userId` is omitted it resolves the current user via `supabase.auth.getUser()` internally, so in a layout you just call `checkUserPermission('module', 'view')`.
- `moduleName` and `action` are typed unions from `@/types/rbac`. IMPORTANT: `'checklists'` is ALREADY a registered ModuleName (src/types/rbac.ts line 63: `| 'checklists'`). `'view'` is a valid ActionType (line 67). So `await checkUserPermission('checklists', 'view')` type-checks today with no rbac.ts change needed. (Other actions available if you need them for create/edit: 'create', 'edit', 'delete', 'manage', etc.)

redirect('/unauthorized'): `redirect` from `next/navigation` throws a NEXT_REDIRECT control-flow signal, so it never returns; place it directly after the failed check and the rest of the component does not execute. This is the standard gate used across the cashing-up section (gate once at the layout level so every child page under it is protected).

Copy-ready checklists gate:
```tsx
const canView = await checkUserPermission('checklists', 'view')
if (!canView) redirect('/unauthorized')
```

answers.q: (1) A COMPLETE representative server action showing permission check + createAdminClient + mutation + revalidatePath + {success/error} return
(2) getOpenSessions verbatim (src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:314-339) and how FohClockWidget.tsx consumes it
(3) Which requested components are exported from @/ds (grep of src/ds barrels)
answers.answer:
`src/ds/index.ts` re-exports everything from `./primitives`, `./composites`, `./icons`, `./tokens`, `./shell`, `./compat`. Every component you listed is exported from `@/ds`. Confirmed presence and source barrel:

FROM @/ds (all import as `import { X } from '@/ds'`):
- Button — primitives (Button.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- Input — primitives (Input.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- Field — primitives (Field.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- Textarea — primitives (Textarea.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- Modal — primitives (Modal.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- ModalActions — compat (compat/ModalActions.tsx) — CONFIRMED (note: it lives in the compat layer, still exported from @/ds)
- Card — composites (Card.tsx); also exports CardHeader, CardBody, CardFooter — CONFIRMED
- Badge — primitives (Badge.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- Table — composites (Table.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- TableHeader — composites (Table.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- TableRow — composites (Table.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- TableCell — composites (Table.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- Select — primitives (Select.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- toast — primitives (Toast.tsx) — CONFIRMED (it is a `{ success, error, warning, info, loading, dismiss }` object, NOT the raw react-hot-toast default; see Q4)
- Alert — primitives (Alert.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- Section — composites (Section.tsx) — CONFIRMED
- Tabs — composites (Tabs.tsx) — CONFIRMED

Exact barrel lines: `src/ds/composites/index.ts:` `export { Table, TableHeader, TableBody, TableRow, TableHead, TableCell, TablePagination } from './Table'` — so the full Table family available is Table, TableHeader, TableBody, TableRow, TableHead, TableCell, TablePagination (you asked for TableHeader/TableRow/TableCell — all present; note it is TableHead for the `<th>` cell and TableHeader for the `<thead>` wrapper).

BONUS components also on @/ds you will likely want: ConfirmDialog, Drawer, Checkbox, Radio, Switch, Spinner, SearchInput, Dropdown/DropdownItem, Tooltip, FileUpload, IconButton, LinkButton, Accordion, Pagination, FormSubmitButton, Empty (primitives); PageHeader, PageLayout, Segmented, DataTable (+`Column` type), DescriptionList, RowActions (composites); and compat: FormGroup, Form/FormSection/FormActions, RadioGroup, EmptyState, StatGroup, FilterPanel, Container, CardTitle/CardDescription, TabNav, BackButton, SortableHeader, DrawerActions.

Select props (src/ds/primitives/Select.tsx) — it extends `React.SelectHTMLAttributes<HTMLSelectElement>` and adds: `label?`, `error?: string | boolean`, `hint?`, `options?: { value: string; label: string }[]`, `placeholder?`. So you can drive it declaratively with `options={[...]}` + `placeholder` instead of child `<option>`s.

answers.q: (4) toast import + usage pattern

(1) How to read business_hours + special_hours for a date (getKitchenWindowForDate: the two maybeSingle queries and day_of_week derivation), verbatim, for building resolveTradingWindow reading opens/closes/is_closed
(2) The canonical rota_published_shifts read with the 3-predicate filter (.eq status scheduled, is_open_shift false, not employee_id null) from real code, plus column names
answers.answer:
File: /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/src/lib/rota/send-rota-emails.ts (lines 48-56). This is the canonical per-employee scheduled-shift read. The three predicates are `.eq('is_open_shift', false)`, `.eq('status', 'scheduled')`, `.not('employee_id', 'is', null)`, additionally scoped by `.eq('week_id', weekId)`:
```ts
supabase
  .from('rota_published_shifts')
  .select('employee_id, shift_date, start_time, end_time, department, name, is_open_shift')
  .eq('week_id', weekId)
  .eq('is_open_shift', false)
  .eq('status', 'scheduled')
  .not('employee_id', 'is', null)
  .order('shift_date')
  .order('start_time'),
```

Column names (snake_case, as selected): `employee_id`, `shift_date`, `start_time`, `end_time`, `department`, `name`, `is_open_shift`, plus filter columns `week_id`, `status`. The `name` column is the template/shift name (nullable). `start_time`/`end_time` are `HH:mm`-style time strings.

The TypeScript row shape used for this select is `ShiftRow` (same file, lines 26-34):
```ts
type ShiftRow = {
  employee_id: string;
  shift_date: string;
  start_time: string;
  end_time: string;
  department: string;
  name: string | null;
  is_open_shift: boolean;
};
```

The complementary open-shifts read (lines 57-64 of the same file) inverts one predicate — `.eq('is_open_shift', true)` and drops the `employee_id` not-null filter, keeping `.eq('status', 'scheduled')`:
```ts
supabase
  .from('rota_published_shifts')
  .select('shift_date, start_time, end_time, department, name')
  .eq('week_id', weekId)
  .eq('is_open_shift', true)
  .eq('status', 'scheduled')
  .order('shift_date')
  .order('start_time'),
```
